question: A team has a pipeline with three sequential stages: Build, Test, and Deploy. The Test stage contains 40 unit tests and 5 integration tests that all run serially. Builds are taking 18 minutes end-to-end. What is the single most effective structural change to reduce pipeline duration?
choices: Split the pipeline into separate pipelines for unit and integration tests
Parallelize the test jobs within the Test stage so they run concurrently
Move the Deploy stage before the Test stage to start deployment sooner
Remove the integration tests to reduce the total number of tests
correct: Parallelize the test jobs within the Test stage so they run concurrently
explanation: Running jobs in parallel within a stage is the canonical technique for reducing wall-clock pipeline time without sacrificing coverage. Splitting into separate pipelines (A) adds coordination overhead and breaks traceability. Deploying before tests pass (C) violates the fundamental CI/CD principle that no artifact reaches an environment without passing its quality gate. Removing integration tests (D) trades speed for risk, not a structural improvement. (Humble & Farley, 'Continuous Delivery', ch. 3)
topic: Pipeline stages, jobs, steps hierarchy, parallelism, and failure behavior

question: A pipeline builds a compiled binary in Stage 1 and needs to run smoke tests against that binary in Stage 3. Stage 2 runs in a different container. The binary is not present in Stage 3. What is the correct mechanism to make it available?
choices: Rebuild the binary inside Stage 3 using the same build command
Declare the binary as a pipeline artifact in Stage 1 and reference it in Stage 3
Use a shared environment variable to pass the binary path between stages
Mount the Stage 1 container's filesystem as a volume in Stage 3
correct: Declare the binary as a pipeline artifact in Stage 1 and reference it in Stage 3
explanation: Artifacts are the mechanism for passing build outputs between stages that run in isolated environments. Each stage gets a clean workspace; artifacts are explicitly archived by the producing stage and restored by consuming stages. Rebuilding (A) introduces non-determinism — the second build might differ from the one that passed tests. Environment variables (C) carry strings, not binary files. Volume-mounting stage containers (D) is not a supported primitive in CI systems and would break isolation. (GitLab CI docs: https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/ci/jobs/job_artifacts.html)
topic: Pipeline stages, jobs, steps hierarchy, parallelism, and failure behavior

question: Your team wants the pipeline to run automatically when code is merged to `main`, nightly at 02:00 UTC regardless of commits, and also on demand by the release manager. Which combination of trigger types covers all three requirements?
choices: Push trigger, pull-request trigger, and a webhook trigger
Push trigger, scheduled trigger, and a manual/API trigger
Tag trigger, scheduled trigger, and a push trigger
Pull-request trigger, scheduled trigger, and a manual/API trigger
correct: Push trigger, scheduled trigger, and a manual/API trigger
explanation: The three requirements map directly to: (1) push trigger — fires on commits landing in `main`; (2) scheduled trigger — cron-based, time-driven execution; (3) manual/API trigger — operator-initiated run on demand. Pull-request triggers (A, D) fire on open/update of a PR, not on merge-to-main. A tag trigger (C) fires only when a Git tag is pushed, not on every commit to `main`. (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/events-that-trigger-workflows)
topic: Pipeline stages, jobs, steps hierarchy, parallelism, and failure behavior

question: A pipeline has five independent jobs (A, B, C, D, E) in the same stage. Job C consistently fails with a transient network error. The `fail-fast` option is enabled. What happens to jobs D and E when C fails?
choices: D and E complete normally because they are independent of C
D and E are immediately cancelled because fail-fast cancels all sibling jobs on first failure
D and E are retried automatically up to three times before cancellation
D and E are moved to the next stage and run there instead
correct: D and E are immediately cancelled because fail-fast cancels all sibling jobs on first failure
explanation: When fail-fast is enabled, the first job failure triggers immediate cancellation of all still-running or queued jobs in the same group. This is intentional — it conserves runner resources and surfaces the failure signal quickly. The jobs are independent in terms of data flow, but fail-fast is a policy applied at the stage/matrix level, not per dependency. Automatic retries (C) are a separate, configurable option. Moving jobs to the next stage (D) is not a behavior any CI system implements. (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions#jobsjob_idstrategyfail-fast)
topic: Pipeline stages, jobs, steps hierarchy, parallelism, and failure behavior

question: A JavaScript project's pipeline spends 4 of its 8 minutes downloading `node_modules` on every run, even when `package-lock.json` has not changed. What is the correct fix?
choices: Pin all dependencies to exact versions in package.json
Configure pipeline dependency caching keyed on the hash of package-lock.json
Install dependencies in a pre-stage that runs once per week on a schedule
Commit node_modules to the repository so it is available without installation
correct: Configure pipeline dependency caching keyed on the hash of package-lock.json
explanation: Dependency caching restores the `node_modules` directory from a previous run when the lock file hash is unchanged, reducing install time from minutes to seconds. The cache key is intentionally tied to the lock file — a change in dependencies automatically invalidates the cache, ensuring correctness. Pinning versions (A) improves reproducibility but does not eliminate the download time. Running install weekly (C) risks stale or missing packages for mid-week pipeline runs. Committing node_modules (D) balloons repository size, causes merge conflicts, and is an anti-pattern explicitly discouraged by Node.js conventions. (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/caching-dependencies-to-speed-up-workflows)
topic: Pipeline stages, jobs, steps hierarchy, parallelism, and failure behavior

question: What is the defining characteristic of a pipeline-as-code approach, compared to a pipeline configured through a GUI?
choices: Pipeline definitions are faster to execute because they bypass the CI server's scheduler
Pipeline definitions live in version control alongside the source code, making them reviewable and auditable
Pipeline definitions are written in a general-purpose programming language, not YAML
Pipeline definitions are owned by the operations team, not the development team
correct: Pipeline definitions live in version control alongside the source code, making them reviewable and auditable
explanation: Pipeline-as-code means the pipeline configuration is a first-class artifact stored in the repo (e.g., Jenkinsfile, .gitlab-ci.yml, .github/workflows/*.yml). This enables code review of pipeline changes, rollback via git history, and branch-level pipeline isolation. Execution speed is unrelated to where the configuration lives (A). Pipeline-as-code is typically YAML or a DSL, not necessarily a general-purpose language (C). Ownership moves toward the team that owns the code, not away from them (D). (Humble & Farley, 'Continuous Delivery', ch. 3; Jenkins docs: https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/pipeline/pipeline-as-code/)
topic: Pipeline stages, jobs, steps hierarchy, parallelism, and failure behavior

question: A fan-out/fan-in pattern in a pipeline means:
choices: One job triggers multiple parallel jobs, whose results are aggregated before a downstream job continues
One stage fans out to multiple pipelines, each in a separate repository
Multiple triggers fan in to a single pipeline run that processes them sequentially
A build artifact fans out to multiple artifact repositories for redundancy
correct: One job triggers multiple parallel jobs, whose results are aggregated before a downstream job continues
explanation: Fan-out is the act of spawning multiple parallel jobs from a single upstream job or stage (e.g., running tests on Linux, macOS, and Windows in parallel). Fan-in is the synchronization point where all those parallel jobs must complete before execution continues to the next stage. This pattern maximizes parallelism while preserving a deterministic ordering guarantee. The other options conflate unrelated concepts: multi-repo triggering (B) is a different orchestration pattern, multiple triggers (C) describes event aggregation not pipeline topology, and artifact replication (D) is a distribution concern. (Humble & Farley, 'Continuous Delivery', ch. 5)
topic: Pipeline stages, jobs, steps hierarchy, parallelism, and failure behavior

question: During a pipeline run, Stage 2 (integration tests) fails. Stage 3 is a notification job that must always send a Slack alert whether Stage 2 passes or fails. Which pipeline behavior must be configured to ensure Stage 3 still runs?
choices: Set Stage 3's dependency to 'none' so it runs unconditionally from the start
Configure Stage 3 to run with an 'always' or equivalent condition that ignores prior stage status
Duplicate Stage 3 as the first stage so it always runs before any failures can occur
Mark Stage 2 as 'allow failure' so the pipeline never reports a failure state
correct: Configure Stage 3 to run with an 'always' or equivalent condition that ignores prior stage status
explanation: CI systems (GitHub Actions `if: always()`, Jenkins `post { always { } }`, GitLab CI `when: always`) allow individual jobs to declare that they run regardless of upstream failure. This is the correct tool for notification and cleanup jobs that must fire on both success and failure paths. Removing the dependency entirely (A) would cause Stage 3 to start in parallel with Stage 1, before it has meaningful results to report. Duplicating the stage (C) adds noise and reports before the pipeline is complete. Marking Stage 2 as allow-failure (D) hides the real failure status — a valid pattern elsewhere but wrong if the intent is to surface the failure in Slack. (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/expressions#always; Jenkins docs: https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/pipeline/syntax/#post)
topic: Pipeline stages, jobs, steps hierarchy, parallelism, and failure behavior

question: A step within a job is different from a job within a stage. Which statement correctly distinguishes them?
choices: Steps run on separate machines; jobs share a single machine
Steps run sequentially within a single job environment; jobs can run in parallel across multiple runners
Steps can define their own triggers; jobs inherit the pipeline trigger
Steps have access to secrets; jobs do not, for security isolation
correct: Steps run sequentially within a single job environment; jobs can run in parallel across multiple runners
explanation: Steps are the smallest unit of work and execute serially within the same runner process, sharing the same filesystem and environment variables. Jobs are the schedulable unit — each job gets its own runner (or container), which means they are isolated from each other and can run in parallel. This means artifacts must be explicitly passed between jobs, whereas steps in the same job share state naturally. The other options invert or incorrectly describe the relationship. (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions#jobsjob_idsteps)
topic: Pipeline stages, jobs, steps hierarchy, parallelism, and failure behavior

question: A pipeline takes 25 minutes to complete. The breakdown is: 3 min build, 18 min tests (sequential), 4 min deploy. After parallelizing the test suite across 6 runners the test time drops to 4 minutes. What is the new end-to-end pipeline duration, assuming no other changes?
choices: 7 minutes
11 minutes
4 minutes
18 minutes
correct: 11 minutes
explanation: The new total is the sum of the sequential phases: 3 min (build) + 4 min (parallelized tests) + 4 min (deploy) = 11 minutes. Build and deploy cannot be parallelized with each other in a standard linear pipeline — they depend on their predecessors completing. Only the test phase was parallelized. Option A (7 min) ignores the deploy phase. Option C (4 min) counts only the tests. Option D (18 min) is the original test time, showing no improvement. (Humble & Farley, 'Continuous Delivery', ch. 5 — commit stage optimization)
topic: Pipeline stages, jobs, steps hierarchy, parallelism, and failure behavior

question: A GitHub Actions workflow must run on every push to any branch AND on every pull request targeting `main`. Which `on:` block correctly expresses this?
choices: `on: [push, pull_request]` with no branch filters
`on: push: branches: ['**']` and `on: pull_request: branches: [main]`
`on: push` and `on: pull_request: branches: [main]` as two sibling keys
`on: workflow_dispatch` with `inputs: branch` to accept both cases at runtime
correct: `on: push: branches: ['**']` and `on: pull_request: branches: [main]`
explanation: The GitHub Actions `on:` block accepts a mapping where each trigger type has its own filter configuration. `push: branches: ['**']` matches all branches using a glob; `pull_request: branches: [main]` restricts the PR trigger to PRs whose base is `main`. Option A (`on: [push, pull_request]`) is valid shorthand that fires on all pushes and all PRs (not only those targeting main), which is broader than required. Option C is syntactically incorrect — `on:` is a single mapping key, not repeatable. Option D (`workflow_dispatch`) is manual-only and does not fire automatically on push or PR events. (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/events-that-trigger-workflows#push)
topic: GitHub Actions workflow structure, triggers, and job configuration

question: A GitHub Actions workflow has three jobs: `build`, `test`, and `deploy`. `test` should only start after `build` succeeds, and `deploy` should only start after `test` succeeds. Which configuration correctly enforces this ordering?
choices: List the jobs in the YAML file in the order build → test → deploy; GitHub Actions runs them in file order
Add `needs: [build]` to `test` and `needs: [test]` to `deploy`
Add `depends_on: build` to `test` and `depends_on: test` to `deploy`
Set `runs-on: sequential` on each job to force serial execution
correct: Add `needs: [build]` to `test` and `needs: [test]` to `deploy`
explanation: The `needs:` keyword is the GitHub Actions mechanism for expressing job dependencies. A job with `needs: [build]` will not start until the listed jobs complete successfully. Without `needs:`, all jobs run in parallel by default, regardless of their order in the YAML file (A is wrong). `depends_on:` (C) is not a valid GitHub Actions key — it exists in GitLab CI syntax, a common confusion. `runs-on:` (D) specifies the runner label, not execution order. (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/workflow-syntax-for-github-actions#jobsjob_idneeds)
topic: GitHub Actions workflow structure, triggers, and job configuration

question: A team needs to test their Node.js application against versions 16, 18, and 20 in parallel without writing three separate jobs. Which GitHub Actions feature should they use?
choices: Reusable workflows called three times with different `with:` inputs
A matrix strategy with `node-version: [16, 18, 20]`
Three separate workflow files, one per Node.js version
A `for` loop inside a `run:` step that installs and tests each version sequentially
correct: A matrix strategy with `node-version: [16, 18, 20]`
explanation: Matrix strategy automatically generates a job run for each combination of values in the matrix. With a single job definition, `strategy: matrix: node-version: [16, 18, 20]` produces three parallel jobs that each receive a different `node-version` context variable. Reusable workflows (A) are a valid but heavier abstraction intended for sharing logic across repositories or workflows, not for simple multi-version fans. Three workflow files (C) are unnecessary duplication and run sequentially by default since each is a separate pipeline. A for-loop in a run step (D) would test sequentially in a single job, losing the parallelism benefit. (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-jobs/using-a-matrix-for-your-jobs)
topic: GitHub Actions workflow structure, triggers, and job configuration

question: A workflow step needs to read the value of a repository secret named `DEPLOY_TOKEN`. Which syntax correctly injects it as an environment variable named `TOKEN`?
choices: `env: TOKEN: $DEPLOY_TOKEN`
`env: TOKEN: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_TOKEN }}`
`env: TOKEN: secrets['DEPLOY_TOKEN']`
`env: TOKEN: {{ env.DEPLOY_TOKEN }}`
correct: `env: TOKEN: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_TOKEN }}`
explanation: GitHub Actions expressions use the `${{ }}` syntax to reference context objects. The `secrets` context exposes repository and environment secrets. The result is masked in logs automatically. Option A uses a bare shell variable syntax which would expand in the shell, not resolve from the secrets context. Option C is JavaScript object notation, not valid YAML expression syntax. Option D omits the dollar sign before the braces and references `env` (environment variables), not `secrets`. (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/security-guides/encrypted-secrets)
topic: GitHub Actions workflow structure, triggers, and job configuration

question: What is the functional difference between a GitHub Action (a reusable unit published to the marketplace) and a GitHub Actions workflow?
choices: Actions are written in JavaScript or as Docker containers; workflows are written in Python
An Action is a reusable step-level component; a workflow is a full CI/CD pipeline that orchestrates jobs and can use Actions as steps
Actions run on GitHub-hosted runners; workflows run on self-hosted runners
Actions require a separate GitHub Apps installation; workflows run natively on any repo
correct: An Action is a reusable step-level component; a workflow is a full CI/CD pipeline that orchestrates jobs and can use Actions as steps
explanation: The hierarchy is: workflow → jobs → steps → actions. An Action is the smallest reusable unit — it can be a JavaScript script, a Docker container, or a composite set of steps packaged for reuse. A workflow is the top-level YAML file under `.github/workflows/` that defines when to run and orchestrates one or more jobs. Actions are consumed inside `steps:` using `uses:`. Options A, C, and D each conflate unrelated constraints: workflows support JavaScript and Docker too (A), runner type is not what distinguishes them (C), and Actions require no separate app installation (D). (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/creating-actions/about-custom-actions)
topic: GitHub Actions workflow structure, triggers, and job configuration

question: A team is running hundreds of GitHub Actions workflow minutes per month on GitHub-hosted runners but wants to avoid usage costs for long integration test jobs. They already manage a fleet of powerful bare-metal servers. What is the most appropriate solution?
choices: Cache all dependencies aggressively to reduce total runtime
Register the bare-metal servers as self-hosted runners and target them with `runs-on: self-hosted`
Split the workflow into multiple repositories, each with fewer workflow minutes
Use GitHub Codespaces instead of Actions runners for CI workloads
correct: Register the bare-metal servers as self-hosted runners and target them with `runs-on: self-hosted`
explanation: Self-hosted runners allow teams to execute jobs on their own infrastructure at no GitHub Actions compute cost. The runner agent is installed on the server, which registers with the GitHub repository, and jobs are routed to it via `runs-on: self-hosted` (or a custom label). Caching (A) reduces time but not the per-minute billing rate for GitHub-hosted runners. Splitting repositories (C) spreads costs but does not eliminate them. GitHub Codespaces (D) is a development environment product, not a CI runner replacement, and would also incur separate costs. (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/hosting-your-own-runners/about-self-hosted-runners)
topic: GitHub Actions workflow structure, triggers, and job configuration

question: A `workflow_dispatch` trigger is configured in a GitHub Actions workflow. What does this enable that a `push` trigger does not?
choices: The workflow can run on forks without requiring a secret
An operator can manually start a run from the GitHub UI or via the API with optional input parameters
The workflow automatically runs on a configurable time interval
The workflow can trigger other workflows in other repositories
correct: An operator can manually start a run from the GitHub UI or via the API with optional input parameters
explanation: `workflow_dispatch` exposes the workflow as a manually triggerable endpoint in the GitHub UI (Actions tab → Run workflow button) and via the REST API. It optionally accepts typed input parameters (string, boolean, choice) that the caller provides at dispatch time. `push` fires only on Git push events — an operator cannot initiate it without pushing code. The scheduled trigger (`schedule:`) handles time intervals (C). Cross-repository triggers use `workflow_dispatch` plus the API or `repository_dispatch`, but this is not inherent to `push` vs. `workflow_dispatch` (D). Fork behavior (A) is a separate permissions concern unrelated to trigger type. (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/manually-running-a-workflow)
topic: GitHub Actions workflow structure, triggers, and job configuration

question: A large organization wants to define a standard 'security scan' job once and reuse it in 30 different application repositories' workflows without copy-pasting the YAML. Which GitHub Actions feature is designed for this use case?
choices: A composite Action stored in a shared repository, referenced with `uses:`
A reusable workflow stored in a shared repository, called with `uses:` in another workflow's job
A workflow template stored in `.github/workflow-templates/` of the organization
A custom GitHub App that injects the job into every repository's workflow at runtime
correct: A reusable workflow stored in a shared repository, called with `uses:` in another workflow's job
explanation: Reusable workflows allow an entire workflow (with its own jobs and steps) to be called from another workflow using `jobs.<job_id>.uses: org/repo/.github/workflows/security-scan.yml@main`. This is the correct abstraction when you need to reuse a full job-level unit of work. A composite Action (A) is step-level reuse — it cannot define its own jobs or set `runs-on`. Workflow templates (C) are starter templates shown in the UI when creating a new workflow; they are copied into the repo, not called dynamically, so changes to the template do not propagate. A custom GitHub App (D) would require significant engineering overhead and is not a standard pattern. (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/reusing-workflows)
topic: GitHub Actions workflow structure, triggers, and job configuration

question: A GitHub Actions job is failing intermittently with 'Runner has run out of disk space.' The job builds a large Docker image. The team is using GitHub-hosted `ubuntu-latest` runners. What is the most appropriate remediation?
choices: Switch from `ubuntu-latest` to `windows-latest` which has more disk space
Add a step early in the job to remove pre-installed tools and free disk space before the Docker build
Increase the job timeout to give the runner more time to clean up automatically
Split the Docker build into two separate workflows that each build half the image layers
correct: Add a step early in the job to remove pre-installed tools and free disk space before the Docker build
explanation: GitHub-hosted runners come with many pre-installed tools (Android SDK, .NET, etc.) that consume 20–30 GB of the available disk. For large Docker builds, a common and well-documented technique is to add an early step that removes unused tool directories, reclaiming 10–20 GB before the build starts. `windows-latest` (A) does not have significantly more free disk space. Increasing the timeout (C) does not reclaim disk space — timeouts govern execution duration, not storage. Splitting layers across workflows (D) would break the Docker build dependency chain and is not a valid Docker image construction pattern. (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-github-hosted-runners/about-github-hosted-runners/about-github-hosted-runners#supported-runners-and-hardware-resources)
topic: GitHub Actions workflow structure, triggers, and job configuration

question: A GitHub Actions workflow uses `on: pull_request:` to run tests. A contributor opens a PR from a fork. The tests fail because `secrets.DEPLOY_TOKEN` is empty. Why, and what is the correct fix?
choices: Fork PRs have secrets stripped for security; fix by granting the fork write access to the repository
Fork PRs cannot access repository secrets by default; the test should be restructured to not require the deployment secret, or use the `pull_request_target` trigger with caution
The secret name is wrong; fork PRs require secrets prefixed with `FORK_`
Secrets are only available in the `main` branch workflow files; the fork must merge to main first
correct: Fork PRs cannot access repository secrets by default; the test should be restructured to not require the deployment secret, or use the `pull_request_target` trigger with caution
explanation: GitHub restricts secret access for workflows triggered by `pull_request` events from forks to prevent secret exfiltration by malicious PRs. The correct architectural fix is to separate test jobs (which run in the fork context, no secrets needed) from deployment jobs (which run in the base repo context after merge). `pull_request_target` runs in the base repo context and can access secrets but must be used very carefully to avoid running untrusted code with elevated permissions. Granting the fork write access (A) is a serious security risk. Secret name prefixes (C) do not exist as a mechanism. Secrets are not branch-restricted (D) — they are pull-event-context-restricted. (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/security-guides/security-hardening-for-github-actions#understanding-the-risk-of-script-injections)
topic: GitHub Actions workflow structure, triggers, and job configuration

question: In a Jenkins Declarative Pipeline, an engineer writes `agent { docker { image 'node:18' } }` at the top-level `pipeline` block. What is the effect?
choices: Each stage runs in its own freshly pulled node:18 container
The entire pipeline runs inside a node:18 Docker container, with each stage executing within that container
Only stages that explicitly reference node:18 use the container; others run on the bare Jenkins agent
Jenkins pulls node:18 once during setup but runs all stages directly on the host filesystem
correct: The entire pipeline runs inside a node:18 Docker container, with each stage executing within that container
explanation: When `agent` is declared at the top-level `pipeline` block in Declarative syntax, it applies to all stages unless a stage overrides it with its own `agent` directive. The Docker agent starts the specified container and runs all stages' `steps` within it. Option A describes a scenario where a stage-level `agent` is declared per-stage, which would spin up a new container per stage. Option C is incorrect — the top-level agent applies globally. Option D mischaracterizes how Docker agents work; the workspace is mounted into the container, not run on the bare host. (Jenkins docs: https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/pipeline/syntax/#agent)
topic: Jenkins Declarative Pipeline and GitLab CI YAML structure and configuration

question: What is the most significant operational difference between Jenkins Declarative Pipeline syntax and Scripted Pipeline syntax?
choices: Declarative pipelines can only run on Linux; Scripted pipelines are cross-platform
Declarative syntax provides a structured, opinionated DSL with validation; Scripted syntax is a Groovy script with full programmatic flexibility but less guardrails
Scripted pipelines are faster because they skip the Declarative syntax parser
Declarative pipelines require the Blue Ocean plugin; Scripted pipelines work with core Jenkins
correct: Declarative syntax provides a structured, opinionated DSL with validation; Scripted syntax is a Groovy script with full programmatic flexibility but less guardrails
explanation: Declarative Pipeline introduced a strict, human-readable structure (`pipeline { agent {} stages { stage { steps {} } } }`) that Jenkins validates before execution, surfacing configuration errors early. Scripted Pipeline is a full Groovy script starting with `node { }` — it can express arbitrary logic but provides no structural validation, making it harder to lint and review. Platform support (A) is identical. Parser overhead (C) is negligible and not a design criterion. Blue Ocean (D) works with both syntaxes and is optional for both. Jenkins currently recommends Declarative for most use cases. (Jenkins docs: https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/pipeline/syntax/#declarative-pipeline)
topic: Jenkins Declarative Pipeline and GitLab CI YAML structure and configuration

question: A Jenkins Declarative Pipeline needs to send a Slack notification after every stage, regardless of whether the stage succeeded or failed, but only run cleanup scripts when the pipeline fails. Which `post` conditions should be used?
choices: `always` for Slack, `failure` for cleanup
`success` for Slack, `failure` for cleanup
`always` for both Slack and cleanup
`unstable` for Slack, `aborted` for cleanup
correct: `always` for Slack, `failure` for cleanup
explanation: Jenkins Declarative Pipeline's `post` block supports conditions: `always` runs regardless of build status, `success` runs only on green builds, `failure` runs only when the build fails, `unstable` runs when the build is unstable (test failures without a build error), and `aborted` runs when manually aborted. For a Slack notification that fires on every outcome, `always` is correct. For cleanup that only runs on failure, `failure` is correct. Using `success` for Slack (B) would miss failure notifications. Using `always` for cleanup (C) would run it even on successful builds, which was not the requirement. `unstable` and `aborted` (D) cover only a subset of outcomes. (Jenkins docs: https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/pipeline/syntax/#post)
topic: Jenkins Declarative Pipeline and GitLab CI YAML structure and configuration

question: In a `.gitlab-ci.yml`, a team has defined `stages: [build, test, deploy]` but their `deploy` job consistently runs at the same time as `test` instead of after it. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The `deploy` job is missing a `needs:` directive referencing the test job
The `deploy` job does not have `stage: deploy` specified, so it defaults to the `test` stage
The `stages:` array order is not respected by GitLab CI; jobs must use `dependencies:` to order themselves
The GitLab runner is configured with `concurrent: 1`, which runs all jobs in parallel to maximize throughput
correct: The `deploy` job does not have `stage: deploy` specified, so it defaults to the `test` stage
explanation: In GitLab CI, every job must declare `stage: <name>` to be assigned to a stage. If a job omits the `stage:` key, it is assigned to the default stage, which is `test`. If the deploy job is running alongside test jobs, the most likely explanation is that it is implicitly in the `test` stage. The `stages:` array ordering is respected by GitLab CI (C is wrong). `needs:` (A) is used for DAG-style out-of-order execution and is a valid tool, but the base issue here is missing stage assignment. `concurrent: 1` on a runner limits parallelism to one job at a time on that runner, which would serialize, not parallelize, jobs (D is the opposite of the described behavior). (GitLab CI docs: https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/ci/yaml/#stage)
topic: Jenkins Declarative Pipeline and GitLab CI YAML structure and configuration

question: A GitLab CI pipeline has a `deploy-prod` job that should require a human to click 'Approve' before it runs. Which configuration achieves this?
choices: Set `rules: - if: '$CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == main'` on the deploy-prod job
Set `when: manual` on the deploy-prod job
Set `allow_failure: false` on the deploy-prod job
Set `environment: production` and `protect: true` on the deploy-prod job
correct: Set `when: manual` on the deploy-prod job
explanation: `when: manual` causes GitLab CI to pause the pipeline at that job and display a play button in the UI that requires a user with the appropriate role to click it. The pipeline does not proceed past this job until the approval action is taken. `rules: if:` (A) controls whether a job is included in the pipeline at all, not whether it needs human approval to run. `allow_failure: false` (C) is the default and causes a job failure to block downstream jobs, but does not add a manual gate before the job. `environment: production` (D) associates the job with a GitLab environment for tracking and deployment history, but `protect: true` is not a valid job-level key — environment protection is configured in the GitLab UI. (GitLab CI docs: https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/ci/yaml/#when)
topic: Jenkins Declarative Pipeline and GitLab CI YAML structure and configuration

question: A platform team maintains a standard set of GitLab CI job templates (lint, SAST, unit-test) in a central repository. Application teams need to consume these without copy-pasting YAML into every project. Which GitLab CI feature enables this?
choices: `extends:` keyword pointing to the central repository's job definitions
`include:` keyword referencing the template file from the central repository
`trigger:` keyword to invoke the central repository's pipeline as a child pipeline
`inherit:` keyword to inherit all job definitions from the parent group's CI configuration
correct: `include:` keyword referencing the template file from the central repository
explanation: `include:` allows a `.gitlab-ci.yml` to pull in YAML from external sources: local files, other project files (via `include: project:`), remote URLs, or GitLab's built-in templates. Application teams add a single `include:` directive to their `.gitlab-ci.yml` and immediately get the platform team's jobs. `extends:` (A) is used for job-level template inheritance within the same file or included files — it cannot pull a file from another repository by itself. `trigger:` (C) starts a downstream pipeline but does not share job definitions. `inherit:` (D) controls whether global `default:` and `variables:` are inherited by jobs, not which jobs are imported. (GitLab CI docs: https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/ci/yaml/includes.html)
topic: Jenkins Declarative Pipeline and GitLab CI YAML structure and configuration

question: A Jenkins pipeline job is taking 45 minutes because it downloads Maven dependencies from the internet on every run. The Jenkins controller and agents run on Kubernetes pods that are ephemeral. What is the most resilient fix?
choices: Use a persistent volume claim mounted at `~/.m2` that survives pod restarts
Add the Maven repository credentials to the Jenkinsfile and download from a faster mirror
Convert the pipeline to Declarative syntax, which has built-in dependency caching
Increase the pod resource limits so Maven downloads faster
correct: Use a persistent volume claim mounted at `~/.m2` that survives pod restarts
explanation: On ephemeral Kubernetes pods, the local filesystem is lost when the pod terminates. Mounting a Persistent Volume Claim (PVC) at the Maven local repository path (`~/.m2/repository`) ensures cached dependencies survive between pipeline runs. This is the Kubernetes-native equivalent of pipeline dependency caching. Switching mirrors (B) reduces download time marginally but does not eliminate repeated downloads. Declarative syntax (C) has no built-in caching mechanism — caching must be explicitly configured regardless of syntax style. Increasing CPU/memory (D) does not significantly affect network download speed, which is the bottleneck. (Jenkins docs: https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/installing/kubernetes/)
topic: Jenkins Declarative Pipeline and GitLab CI YAML structure and configuration

question: In GitLab CI, what is the `environment:` keyword used for when applied to a deployment job?
choices: It sets OS-level environment variables that are injected into the job's shell session
It associates the job with a named GitLab environment, enabling deployment tracking, environment-scoped variables, and review app management
It specifies which GitLab runner environment (Docker, Shell, Kubernetes) to use for the job
It defines the infrastructure environment (dev, staging, prod) where the runner is physically hosted
correct: It associates the job with a named GitLab environment, enabling deployment tracking, environment-scoped variables, and review app management
explanation: The `environment:` keyword links a CI job to a GitLab Environment object. This creates a deployment history record, enables environment-specific protected variables, shows live deployment status on the Environments page, and supports review apps (ephemeral environments per branch). It does not set shell environment variables (A) — those use `variables:`. It does not select the runner executor type (C) — that is configured on the runner itself. It does not describe physical infrastructure location (D). (GitLab CI docs: https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/ci/environments/)
topic: Jenkins Declarative Pipeline and GitLab CI YAML structure and configuration

question: A Jenkins Declarative Pipeline needs the `build` stage to run on any available agent, but the `deploy` stage must run on an agent with the label `production-agent`. How is this expressed?
choices: Set `agent any` at the pipeline level and add `agent { label 'production-agent' }` inside the `deploy` stage
Set `agent { label 'production-agent' }` at the pipeline level; it applies selectively only to the deploy stage
Use `environment { AGENT = 'production-agent' }` inside the deploy stage
Use a `when { branch 'production' }` condition to route the deploy stage to the correct agent
correct: Set `agent any` at the pipeline level and add `agent { label 'production-agent' }` inside the `deploy` stage
explanation: Jenkins Declarative Pipeline supports both a top-level `agent` and per-stage `agent` overrides. Setting `agent any` at the top level means stages without an explicit agent use any available node. Adding `agent { label 'production-agent' }` inside a specific stage overrides the global setting for that stage only, ensuring it runs on an agent matching the label. Setting a specific agent globally (B) would route all stages to that label, not selectively. `environment:` (C) sets variables, not routing. `when { branch 'production' }` (D) controls whether the stage runs based on branch name, not which agent it runs on. (Jenkins docs: https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/pipeline/syntax/#agent)
topic: Jenkins Declarative Pipeline and GitLab CI YAML structure and configuration

question: A GitLab CI pipeline has both `fast-tests` and `slow-tests` jobs in the `test` stage. The `deploy` job normally runs after the `test` stage. A team wants `deploy` to start as soon as `fast-tests` passes, without waiting for `slow-tests`. Which GitLab CI feature enables this?
choices: Set `allow_failure: true` on `slow-tests` so it does not block `deploy`
Use `needs: [fast-tests]` on the `deploy` job to declare a DAG dependency, bypassing the stage ordering
Move `slow-tests` to a separate stage after `deploy`
Use `parallel: 2` on the test stage to run both jobs simultaneously and halve the wait time
correct: Use `needs: [fast-tests]` on the `deploy` job to declare a DAG dependency, bypassing the stage ordering
explanation: `needs:` in GitLab CI enables Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) execution, where a job can start as soon as its explicitly listed dependencies complete, ignoring stage boundaries. With `needs: [fast-tests]`, `deploy` starts immediately when `fast-tests` passes, while `slow-tests` continues in parallel. `allow_failure: true` on `slow-tests` (A) would let the pipeline continue past a slow-tests failure, but deploy would still wait for the full test stage to finish. Moving slow-tests to a post-deploy stage (C) would run slow tests after deploying, reversing the quality gate. `parallel:` (D) is a keyword for splitting a single job across multiple runners, not for controlling job ordering. (GitLab CI docs: https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/ci/directed_acyclic_graph/)
topic: Jenkins Declarative Pipeline and GitLab CI YAML structure and configuration

question: According to the test pyramid, why should unit tests form the largest tier and end-to-end tests the smallest tier in a CI pipeline?
choices: Unit tests are easier to write, so teams naturally write more of them regardless of value
Unit tests are fast, isolated, and cheap to run and maintain; end-to-end tests are slow, brittle, and expensive, so a small suite catches only the highest-value user flows
CI systems can parallelize unit tests but cannot parallelize end-to-end tests, forcing the pyramid shape
End-to-end tests require a production environment which CI pipelines cannot access
correct: Unit tests are fast, isolated, and cheap to run and maintain; end-to-end tests are slow, brittle, and expensive, so a small suite catches only the highest-value user flows
explanation: The test pyramid (coined by Mike Cohn, popularized in Google's Testing Blog) is an economic model: unit tests run in milliseconds, have no external dependencies, and pinpoint failures precisely, making them cheap to run and maintain at scale. End-to-end tests spin up full environments, depend on external services, run in seconds to minutes each, and produce false failures due to flakiness — making a large E2E suite a maintenance burden that slows pipelines without proportional value. The pyramid shape reflects return on investment, not ease of writing (A) or parallelism constraints (C). E2E tests routinely run against staging environments in CI (D is false). (Google Testing Blog: https://testing.googleblog.com/2015/04/just-say-no-to-more-end-to-end-tests.html)
topic: Testing strategies, quality gates, and test execution in CI pipelines

question: A team configures their CI pipeline to fail the build if code coverage drops below 80%. A developer adds a new module without tests, bringing coverage to 76%. The pipeline fails. What type of quality gate has been enforced?
choices: A static analysis gate, because coverage is measured without running the code
A code coverage threshold gate, which uses dynamic test execution results to block merges below a defined minimum
A linting gate, because coverage tools parse source code similarly to linters
A SAST gate, because measuring untested code is equivalent to detecting security vulnerabilities in unexecuted paths
correct: A code coverage threshold gate, which uses dynamic test execution results to block merges below a defined minimum
explanation: Coverage is measured by instrumenting the runtime during test execution (dynamic analysis) and tracking which lines/branches were exercised. A coverage threshold quality gate compares the measured percentage against a configured minimum and fails the build if the threshold is not met. This enforces that new code ships with adequate test coverage. Static analysis (A) does not execute the code — it analyzes source text. Linting (C) is a subset of static analysis focused on style and syntax errors. SAST (D) is Static Application Security Testing, which analyzes code for security vulnerabilities, not for test coverage. (Humble & Farley, 'Continuous Delivery', ch. 8 — automated testing strategy)
topic: Testing strategies, quality gates, and test execution in CI pipelines

question: A CI pipeline runs a SAST tool that scans the source code before any tests execute. What is SAST, and why is placing it early in the pipeline advantageous?
choices: SAST is runtime penetration testing; placing it early catches security bugs before they reach the network
SAST is Static Application Security Testing — it analyzes source code or binaries without executing them, making it fast and able to provide early security feedback before slower dynamic tests run
SAST is a secret scanning tool that detects API keys committed to the repository
SAST is a dependency vulnerability scanner that checks third-party libraries for known CVEs
correct: SAST is Static Application Security Testing — it analyzes source code or binaries without executing them, making it fast and able to provide early security feedback before slower dynamic tests run
explanation: SAST tools (e.g., Semgrep, Checkmarx, SonarQube) analyze code patterns, data flows, and ASTs to identify security vulnerabilities such as SQL injection, XSS, or insecure deserialization without running the application. Because they are static, they are fast and can run in the commit stage alongside unit tests, providing shift-left security feedback. SAST is not runtime penetration testing (A) — that is DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing). Secret scanning (C) is a related but distinct tool category. Dependency CVE scanning (D) is Software Composition Analysis (SCA), another distinct discipline. (GitHub docs: https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/code-scanning/introduction-to-code-scanning/about-code-scanning)
topic: Testing strategies, quality gates, and test execution in CI pipelines

question: A CI pipeline runs 500 end-to-end tests serially. The tests collectively take 40 minutes, and 8% consistently fail with no reproducible local failure (flaky tests). The team is blocking merges on any E2E failure. What is the recommended strategy for handling the flaky tests?
choices: Remove all flaky tests from the suite immediately to unblock the pipeline
Quarantine flaky tests in a separate non-blocking suite, track them, and fix the root cause — usually shared state or timing dependencies
Re-run the entire test suite on any failure, up to five times, until all tests pass
Mark flaky tests as `@ignore` so they are permanently skipped and do not affect the pipeline
correct: Quarantine flaky tests in a separate non-blocking suite, track them, and fix the root cause — usually shared state or timing dependencies
explanation: Flaky tests erode trust in the pipeline and waste engineer time on false failures. The industry-standard approach is quarantine: move flaky tests to a non-blocking suite that still runs (so failures are visible and tracked) but does not gate merges. The team then allocates time to fix the root cause, which is typically shared mutable state, time-dependent assertions, external service dependencies, or race conditions. Deleting them outright (A) discards the test coverage they provided. Retrying the entire suite (C) masks the problem and extends pipeline time. Permanently ignoring (D) is equivalent to deleting with extra steps and leaves the flakiness unfixed. (Google Testing Blog: https://testing.googleblog.com/2016/05/flaky-tests-at-google-and-how-we.html)
topic: Testing strategies, quality gates, and test execution in CI pipelines

question: What distinguishes static analysis from dynamic testing in a CI context?
choices: Static analysis runs in production; dynamic testing runs in staging
Static analysis examines source code or artifacts without executing the program; dynamic testing runs the program and observes runtime behavior
Static analysis is performed by humans in code review; dynamic testing is automated
Static analysis checks for memory leaks; dynamic testing checks for syntax errors
correct: Static analysis examines source code or artifacts without executing the program; dynamic testing runs the program and observes runtime behavior
explanation: The static/dynamic distinction is about whether the code executes. Static analysis tools (linters, SAST, type checkers) parse and analyze code text or compiled artifacts to find patterns — no runtime is needed. Dynamic testing (unit tests, integration tests, DAST) executes the code and observes actual behavior, catching runtime errors, concurrency bugs, and behavior-level regressions that static tools cannot see. Neither is tied to a specific environment (A). Code review is a manual process, not synonymous with static analysis (C). Memory leak detection is dynamic; syntax checking is static — the example in (D) reverses the correct pairing. (Google Testing Blog: https://testing.googleblog.com/)
topic: Testing strategies, quality gates, and test execution in CI pipelines

question: A team wants to enforce that no pull request can merge if a critical SonarQube quality gate fails (e.g., new code introduces a blocker issue). Which CI/CD mechanism makes this enforcement automatic?
choices: A scheduled pipeline that scans all merged code weekly and creates GitHub issues for violations
A branch protection rule requiring a CI status check (the SonarQube scan job) to pass before merge is permitted
A CODEOWNERS file that assigns the security team as required reviewers for all pull requests
A post-merge pipeline that reverts the merge commit if the quality gate fails
correct: A branch protection rule requiring a CI status check (the SonarQube scan job) to pass before merge is permitted
explanation: Branch protection rules (GitHub) or protected branch settings (GitLab) can require that named CI status checks pass before a merge is allowed. The SonarQube CI job reports its quality gate result as a commit status (pass/fail) to the VCS. If the job fails, the merge button is disabled. Weekly scanning (A) is a reactive audit, not a gate — code with violations would already be merged. CODEOWNERS (C) adds human review requirements, not automated quality enforcement. Post-merge revert (D) allows bad code to momentarily land in main and requires a second automated action, increasing risk and churn. (GitHub docs: https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-protected-branches/about-protected-branches)
topic: Testing strategies, quality gates, and test execution in CI pipelines

question: A backend service has 1,200 unit tests and 80 integration tests. The integration tests take 14 minutes total. Which strategy best reduces integration test pipeline time while preserving coverage?
choices: Run only a random 50% of integration tests on each pipeline run to halve the time
Split the integration test suite into groups and run the groups in parallel across multiple runners
Convert all integration tests into unit tests by mocking external dependencies
Run integration tests only on the `main` branch, skipping them on feature branches
correct: Split the integration test suite into groups and run the groups in parallel across multiple runners
explanation: Parallel test execution splits the test suite into shards that run simultaneously, reducing wall-clock time proportionally to the number of runners. With 4 runners, 14 minutes becomes roughly 3.5 minutes with no coverage loss. Random sampling (A) preserves the time reduction but randomly skips tests on each run, meaning failures can be missed — this is not a principled approach to coverage. Converting to unit tests with mocks (C) removes the actual integration coverage that mocks cannot provide. Skipping on feature branches (D) delays feedback until merge, when fixing is more expensive. (Google Testing Blog: https://testing.googleblog.com/2010/06/splitting-tests-at-google.html)
topic: Testing strategies, quality gates, and test execution in CI pipelines

question: A pipeline fails on the integration test stage. The error log says 'connection refused to postgres:5432'. The application code has not changed since the last successful run. What is the most likely cause and first diagnostic step?
choices: A new code change introduced a connection string bug; re-read the application config files
The PostgreSQL service container failed to start or become ready before the tests ran; check the service container logs and add a readiness wait step
The test runner is using the wrong test database; verify the DATABASE_URL environment variable
PostgreSQL 5432 is blocked by a firewall rule that was recently changed; open a network ticket
correct: The PostgreSQL service container failed to start or become ready before the tests ran; check the service container logs and add a readiness wait step
explanation: In CI pipelines that use service containers (GitHub Actions `services:`, GitLab CI `services:`), a common failure mode is the application container starting and running tests before the database container has finished initializing. 'Connection refused' on the expected host/port is the canonical symptom. The first step is to check the service container logs for startup errors. The fix is usually adding a wait/healthcheck loop (e.g., `pg_isready`) before running tests. Because the code has not changed (A is unlikely), a config mismatch (C) would have failed consistently, and a firewall change (D) is possible but requires infrastructure context — the service container explanation is far more common in CI environments. (GitHub Actions docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-containerized-services/about-service-containers)
topic: Testing strategies, quality gates, and test execution in CI pipelines

question: A team's unit test suite passes 100% of the time, and code coverage is at 92%. Yet a critical bug regularly reaches production. Which gap most likely explains this?
choices: The unit tests are not written in the correct testing framework
High coverage measures which lines are executed, not whether the assertions are meaningful — tests may not assert the correct behavior
92% coverage is too low; 100% coverage would catch all bugs
Unit tests cannot catch bugs; only end-to-end tests can detect production issues
correct: High coverage measures which lines are executed, not whether the assertions are meaningful — tests may not assert the correct behavior
explanation: Code coverage is a measure of test execution breadth, not test quality. A test can execute a line with no assertion (a 'vacuous' test) and still count toward coverage. Bugs often hide in edge cases, integration points, or incorrect business logic that is exercised by tests but not validated by assertions. 92% coverage with weak assertions allows bugs to pass. 100% coverage (C) is not achievable in practice and would still not guarantee correctness without meaningful assertions. Framework choice (A) is irrelevant to this problem. Unit tests absolutely can catch bugs (D) — the issue is assertion quality. (Google Testing Blog: https://testing.googleblog.com/2020/08/code-coverage-best-practices.html)
topic: Testing strategies, quality gates, and test execution in CI pipelines

question: A pipeline runs unit tests, integration tests, and a SAST scan on every commit. The SAST scan takes 12 minutes and has no dependency on the test results. How should the pipeline be restructured to minimize time-to-feedback on test failures?
choices: Move the SAST scan to the end of the pipeline so it does not slow down test feedback
Run the SAST scan in parallel with the test jobs so both complete concurrently
Remove the SAST scan from the commit pipeline and run it only on a nightly schedule
Run the SAST scan only on the `main` branch to avoid slowing feature branch pipelines
correct: Run the SAST scan in parallel with the test jobs so both complete concurrently
explanation: Because the SAST scan and tests are independent, they are ideal candidates for parallelization. Running them concurrently means the total pipeline time is max(SAST_time, test_time) rather than the sum, minimizing time-to-feedback without removing any checks. Moving SAST to the end (A) still adds 12 minutes to total pipeline time — it just delays the security signal. Nightly scheduling (C) removes the shift-left security benefit and allows vulnerable code to merge during the day undetected. Branch filtering (D) means most code (on feature branches) is never scanned, which is a significant security gap. (Humble & Farley, 'Continuous Delivery', ch. 3 — commit stage design)
topic: Testing strategies, quality gates, and test execution in CI pipelines

question: A team is preparing to deploy a new version of a critical payments service. They want zero-downtime deployment with the ability to instantly roll back to the previous version by switching traffic, without re-deploying code. Which strategy best satisfies these requirements?
choices: Rolling update — replace instances one by one with the new version
Blue/green deployment — run two identical environments and switch the load balancer between them
Canary deployment — route 5% of traffic to the new version initially
Recreate deployment — terminate all old instances and then start new instances
correct: Blue/green deployment — run two identical environments and switch the load balancer between them
explanation: Blue/green deployment maintains two full, production-identical environments (blue = current, green = new). The new version is deployed to green, validated, and then traffic is cut over by updating the load balancer or DNS. Rollback is instantaneous: flip the load balancer back to blue — no code re-deployment required. Rolling updates (A) replace instances gradually; rollback requires re-deploying the old version. Canary (C) allows gradual validation but does not provide instant rollback — partial traffic still hits the new version during rollback. Recreate (D) causes downtime between termination and startup. (Humble & Farley, 'Continuous Delivery', ch. 10)
topic: Deployment strategies, release patterns, and environment promotion

question: A team deploys a new recommendation engine to 2% of users. After 30 minutes, they observe elevated error rates in the canary cohort. What is the correct next action?
choices: Increase the canary to 10% to gather more data before deciding
Roll back by routing all traffic back to the stable version and investigate the root cause
Hot-patch the canary environment directly in production to fix the issue
Continue the rollout to 100% because 2% errors may not be statistically significant
correct: Roll back by routing all traffic back to the stable version and investigate the root cause
explanation: The purpose of canary deployment is early detection with blast radius limitation. When the canary cohort exhibits elevated error rates, the correct response is to immediately halt the rollout and route all traffic back to the stable version, returning to a known-good state before investigating. Increasing the canary percentage (A) expands the blast radius before the cause is understood. Hot-patching in production (C) bypasses the entire tested delivery pipeline — changes must go through the pipeline. Continuing to 100% (D) would impact all users with a known-bad version. (Humble & Farley, 'Continuous Delivery', ch. 10; Google SRE Book — Release Engineering)
topic: Deployment strategies, release patterns, and environment promotion

question: What is the primary purpose of a feature flag in a CI/CD context?
choices: To enable A/B testing of UI color schemes only
To decouple code deployment from feature release, allowing code to ship to production in a disabled state and be enabled independently of a deployment
To gate access to pipeline jobs based on the committing user's role
To replace environment variables with a more dynamic configuration system
correct: To decouple code deployment from feature release, allowing code to ship to production in a disabled state and be enabled independently of a deployment
explanation: Feature flags (also called feature toggles) allow teams to merge and deploy code to production with the feature disabled. The release — making the feature visible to users — is a separate, often non-technical decision that can be made post-deployment. This enables trunk-based development (merging to main frequently without release risk), dark launches, gradual rollouts, and instant kill-switches. A/B testing (A) is one application of feature flags, not their primary CI/CD purpose. Role-based pipeline gating (C) is an access control concern. Dynamic configuration (D) is a side benefit, not the defining purpose. (Humble & Farley, 'Continuous Delivery', ch. 10 — release strategies)
topic: Deployment strategies, release patterns, and environment promotion

question: A Kubernetes deployment uses a rolling update strategy with `maxUnavailable: 1` and `maxSurge: 1`. The service has 4 replicas. Describe what happens during the update.
choices: All 4 old pods are terminated simultaneously, then 4 new pods are started
One old pod is terminated and one new pod is started at a time, keeping at least 3 replicas serving traffic throughout
Two old pods are replaced at once because maxSurge and maxUnavailable add up to 2
The deployment waits for all 4 new pods to be ready before terminating any old pods
correct: One old pod is terminated and one new pod is started at a time, keeping at least 3 replicas serving traffic throughout
explanation: With `maxUnavailable: 1`, Kubernetes ensures no more than 1 replica is unavailable at any time (so at least 3 of the 4 are serving). With `maxSurge: 1`, it can run at most 5 pods temporarily (4 + 1 surge). The practical effect is: 1 new pod starts (surge), once it's ready, 1 old pod is terminated, and this repeats until all 4 are updated. Option A is the Recreate strategy. Option C misinterprets the parameters — they operate as independent constraints, not additive. Option D describes blue/green, where all new instances are ready before any old ones are removed. (Kubernetes docs: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/#rolling-update-deployment)
topic: Deployment strategies, release patterns, and environment promotion

question: What is trunk-based development, and how does it relate to CI/CD pipeline health?
choices: All developers commit directly to short-lived feature branches that are merged to a trunk (main) at least once per day, keeping the trunk always releasable
A single developer is designated as the 'trunk owner' who merges all PRs to maintain code quality
Trunk-based development requires a monorepo where all services share one codebase
Developers work on long-lived feature branches to avoid breaking the main branch between releases
correct: All developers commit directly to short-lived feature branches that are merged to a trunk (main) at least once per day, keeping the trunk always releasable
explanation: Trunk-based development (TBD) is the practice of integrating to a single shared branch (trunk/main) frequently — ideally daily or more. Short-lived branches (if used at all) are open for hours, not days. This practice is the enabler of true Continuous Integration: if the branch lives longer than a day, integration is not continuous. It keeps the trunk always in a potentially releasable state, which is a prerequisite for Continuous Delivery. Option B describes a process bottleneck, not TBD. Option C conflates TBD with monorepo architecture — they are independent choices. Option D is the opposite of TBD; long-lived branches are the anti-pattern TBD was designed to replace. (Humble & Farley, 'Continuous Delivery', ch. 14; trunkbaseddevelopment.com)
topic: Deployment strategies, release patterns, and environment promotion

question: An application is promoted through three environments: dev → staging → prod. A bug is discovered in staging that was not caught in dev. Which root cause most likely explains this, and what is the architectural fix?
choices: Staging has too many tests; reduce staging test coverage to match dev
Dev and staging are not environment parity — they differ in configuration, data, or infrastructure, allowing bugs that only manifest in staging conditions
The promotion pipeline is running the wrong artifact version in staging; add a version check step
Bugs in staging always indicate flaky tests; quarantine the failing staging tests
correct: Dev and staging are not environment parity — they differ in configuration, data, or infrastructure, allowing bugs that only manifest in staging conditions
explanation: The 'works in dev, fails in staging' pattern is a classic environment parity problem. Dev often runs with mocked services, smaller datasets, different OS versions, or relaxed configuration — conditions that don't reproduce the bug. The fix is increasing parity: use the same Docker images, same configuration management, same infrastructure-as-code templates, and production-representative data in staging. Reducing staging test coverage (A) would make the problem worse. Version mismatch (C) would cause broad failures, not environment-specific bugs. Flaky tests (D) is unrelated to bugs that reliably appear in staging. (Twelve-Factor App methodology, factor X — Dev/prod parity)
topic: Deployment strategies, release patterns, and environment promotion

question: A production deployment fails 10 minutes after cutover. The previous version is still available as a known-good artifact. Which rollback strategy restores service most quickly?
choices: Re-run the full CI pipeline from source to rebuild and redeploy the previous version
Redeploy the previously built and validated artifact directly to production, bypassing the build stage
Revert the Git commit and wait for the pipeline to automatically detect the revert and deploy
Restore from the most recent database backup and restart the current version
correct: Redeploy the previously built and validated artifact directly to production, bypassing the build stage
explanation: The key insight is that the previous artifact was already built, tested, and validated — redeploying it is the fastest path to a known-good state. Rerunning the full pipeline (A) takes the same time as a forward deployment — minutes to tens of minutes — during which the outage continues. Reverting the Git commit (C) requires the pipeline to rebuild, test, and deploy the reverted code, which is nearly as slow as A. Database restoration (D) is the last resort for data corruption scenarios; it discards data written after the backup and should not be the first response to a code deployment failure. Artifact-based rollback is why CI/CD systems version and store build artifacts. (Humble & Farley, 'Continuous Delivery', ch. 10 — rollback strategies)
topic: Deployment strategies, release patterns, and environment promotion

question: A team ships a new API endpoint behind a feature flag. The endpoint is deployed to production but the flag is off for all users. A week later, the product team enables the flag for 10% of users. Which statement correctly describes the relationship between deployment and release here?
choices: Deployment and release happened simultaneously when the code was pushed to production
Deployment happened when the code was shipped to production; release happened when the flag was enabled for users — they are separate events in time
Release happened first (flag configuration) and deployment second (code shipping), reversing the usual order
Because the feature was not visible to users when deployed, no deployment event occurred
correct: Deployment happened when the code was shipped to production; release happened when the flag was enabled for users — they are separate events in time
explanation: This question illustrates the core value proposition of feature flags in CI/CD: decoupling deployment (code reaches the environment) from release (feature becomes visible to users). Deployment is a technical event; release is a product/business decision. They can be separated by days or weeks. Option A assumes they must be simultaneous, which is the old model feature flags are designed to replace. Option C reverses the correct sequence — the flag existed in the codebase as 'off' before it was enabled. Option D incorrectly redefines deployment; code running in production is deployed regardless of whether a flag gates its visibility. (Humble & Farley, 'Continuous Delivery', ch. 10)
topic: Deployment strategies, release patterns, and environment promotion

question: A canary deployment routes 10% of production traffic to a new version. Which metric should trigger an automatic rollback of the canary?
choices: The canary pod's CPU utilization exceeds 70% for 5 minutes
The canary's error rate exceeds the stable version's error rate by more than a defined threshold (e.g., 2x baseline)
The number of requests to the canary falls below the expected 10% of total traffic
The canary deployment has been running for more than 30 minutes without a manual promotion decision
correct: The canary's error rate exceeds the stable version's error rate by more than a defined threshold (e.g., 2x baseline)
explanation: Automated canary rollback should be triggered by user-impacting signals, not resource utilization or time. Error rate relative to the stable baseline is the canonical rollback trigger: if the canary is producing significantly more errors, it indicates a regression. Other signals include latency percentiles (p99 latency increase) and success rate. CPU utilization (A) is a resource metric that may legitimately increase due to new feature load and does not directly indicate user impact. Traffic distribution below 10% (C) is a load balancer issue, not a quality signal about the new version. A time threshold (D) is a valid operational policy for promoting or rolling back a stale canary, but does not reflect software quality — it's an administrative timeout. (Google SRE Book — Release Engineering; Humble & Farley, 'Continuous Delivery', ch. 10)
topic: Deployment strategies, release patterns, and environment promotion

question: A team is evaluating whether to use blue/green deployment or canary deployment for a major database schema migration that is backward-incompatible. Which consideration most strongly favors blue/green over canary for this specific scenario?
choices: Blue/green is cheaper because it requires fewer running instances during the cutover window
Blue/green avoids running two versions of the application simultaneously against the same database, which a backward-incompatible schema migration would break
Canary deployments are only suitable for frontend changes, not backend schema migrations
Blue/green deployments are faster to execute because no traffic-splitting configuration is required
correct: Blue/green avoids running two versions of the application simultaneously against the same database, which a backward-incompatible schema migration would break
explanation: Canary and rolling deployments both run old and new application versions simultaneously for a period. If the database schema migration is backward-incompatible (e.g., a column is renamed or dropped), the old version of the application will fail when it encounters the new schema, or the new version will fail against the old schema. Blue/green mitigates this by migrating the schema before cutover, then flipping all traffic to the new application version at once — the old version is never routed traffic against the new schema. Blue/green requires double the infrastructure, not less (A is wrong). Canary is used for backend services extensively (C is wrong). Traffic-splitting for blue/green still requires load balancer reconfiguration (D is not accurate). (Humble & Farley, 'Continuous Delivery', ch. 12 — data management for deployment)
topic: Deployment strategies, release patterns, and environment promotion
